summary: Exodus exhibits systematic anthropomorphism avoidance across LXX and Targums, particularly in theophanies (Exod 24, 33–34), and proto-rabbinic exegetical expansion in Targumic tradition regarding the golden calf and covenant renewal. The LXX introduces occasional harmonizations with Pentateuchal legal material, while Targums display halakhic precision and angelological intermediation to buffer divine-human contact.
summary_plain: Translators of Exodus softened direct descriptions of God's body and emotions, especially in scenes where Moses meets God. Jewish Aramaic translations added interpretive details to clarify legal rules and inserted angels as go-betweens to avoid saying God appeared directly to people.

revisions.mt_reading: They saw the God of Israel, and under his feet was something like a pavement of sapphire stone
revisions.revised_reading: They saw the place where the God of Israel stood, and under his feet was as it were a work of sapphire stone
revisions.evidence: The LXX inserts τὸν τόπον οὗ ('the place where') before 'the God of Israel stood,' redirecting the vision from God himself to the location of his presence. This is a clear strategy to mitigate the direct visio Dei, consistent with Hellenistic Jewish apologetic concerns about corporeal theism. The addition is syntactically awkward and interrupts the narrative flow, signaling theological intervention rather than organic translation.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek adds 'the place where' God stood, so the elders see a location rather than God himself. This awkward phrasing shows theological discomfort with people seeing God directly.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 389
Rösel, Übersetzung als Vollendung der Auslegung, p. 102
revisions.counter_arguments: Could reflect exegetical tradition that τόπος already carried sacral connotations, making this a clarification rather than avoidance.

revisions.mt_reading: They beheld God and ate and drank
revisions.revised_reading: They beheld the glory of the Shekinah of the Lord and rejoiced in their offerings which were accepted as favorably as if they had eaten and drunk
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos and Pseudo-Jonathan replace direct vision of God with 'glory of the Shekinah,' a standard Targumic buffer term. The extraordinary reinterpretation of eating/drinking as sacrificial acceptance reveals discomfort with the convivial meal in God's presence, replacing physical communion with cultic metaphor. This is programmatic Targumic theology, not translation variance.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translation changes 'saw God' to 'saw God's glory' and turns the shared meal into a metaphor for accepted sacrifices, avoiding the scandalous image of eating with God.
scholarly_citations: Aberbach & Grossfeld, Targum Onkelos to Exodus, p. 157
revisions.counter_arguments: Shekinah terminology might preserve an older tradition of divine manifestation rather than constitute avoidance.

revisions.mt_reading: You cannot see my face, for no human may see me and live
revisions.revised_reading: You cannot see the face of my Shekinah, for no human can see me and live in this world, but they will see me in the world to come
revisions.evidence: Targum Pseudo-Jonathan adds eschatological resolution ('in the world to come') to the prohibition, reflecting proto-rabbinic speculation on beatific vision in the age to come (cf. b. Berakhot 17a). The contrast between 'this world' and 'world to come' is absent in MT and represents halakhic-theological elaboration characteristic of tannaitic exegesis.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic adds that while no one can see God now, people will see God in the afterlife. This reflects early rabbinic thinking about the world to come.
scholarly_citations: McNamara, Targum and Testament Revisited, p. 89
revisions.counter_arguments: The eschatological addition might be homiletical expansion rather than strict theological correction of MT.

revisions.mt_reading: Then I will remove my hand and you will see my back, but my face shall not be seen
revisions.revised_reading: Then I will remove my hand and you will see my back parts, but my face shall not be seen
revisions.evidence: The LXX renders אֲחֹרָי as τὰ ὀπίσω μου ('the things behind me'), using a neuter plural rather than maintaining the anatomical singular. While subtle, this abstracts the corporeal reference. However, the LXX retains χείρ ('hand') and πρόσωπον ('face'), limiting the avoidance strategy. This may reflect compromise between literal rendering and theological sensitivity.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek uses a slightly vaguer phrase 'the things behind me' instead of 'my back,' softening but not eliminating the body language about God.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 547
revisions.counter_arguments: The neuter plural is standard Greek idiom for 'back parts' and may not carry theological intent.

revisions.mt_reading: And YHWH relented (נחם) concerning the evil he said he would do to his people
revisions.revised_reading: And there was regret (נחמתא) before the Lord concerning the evil which he had said (through the angel of death) he would do to his people
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos shifts agency: 'regret before the Lord' (passive divine reception) rather than active divine emotion. Pseudo-Jonathan inserts 'through the angel of death,' externalizing the execution of judgment to an intermediary. Both strategies buffer direct divine mutability and emotional response, core Targumic concerns reflecting early Jewish philosophical theology.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic makes God's 'relenting' more passive and adds that an angel would have carried out the punishment, avoiding the idea that God changes his mind emotionally.
scholarly_citations: Aberbach & Grossfeld, Targum Onkelos to Exodus, p. 172
Hayward, The Targums and the Transmission of Scripture, p. 44
revisions.counter_arguments: The passive construction might be stylistic preference in Aramaic rather than theological avoidance.

revisions.mt_reading: YHWH met him and sought to kill him. Then Zipporah took a flint and cut off her son's foreskin
revisions.revised_reading: An angel of the Lord met him and sought to kill him. Then Zipporah took a stone and cut off her son's foreskin
revisions.evidence: Several LXX manuscripts read ἄγγελος κυρίου ('angel of the Lord') instead of κύριος, externalizing the murderous intent to an intermediary. This notorious crux—God's inexplicable violence against Moses—is theologically smoothed by angelological substitution. Manuscript evidence is mixed, but the tendency is clear: post-MT tradents found the passage offensive.
revisions.evidence_plain: Some Greek manuscripts change 'the Lord' to 'an angel of the Lord' in this disturbing scene where God tries to kill Moses, making the attack less theologically troubling.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 54
revisions.counter_arguments: The angel reading might be original LXX, with κύριος a later harmonization toward MT.

revisions.mt_reading: When the ram's horn sounds a long blast (בִּמְשֹׁךְ הַיֹּבֵל), they may go up the mountain
revisions.revised_reading: When the voices and trumpets and cloud depart from the mountain, they may go up the mountain
revisions.evidence: The LXX expands the single acoustic signal into a comprehensive theophanic departure ('voices, trumpets, cloud'), harmonizing with the fuller description in 19:16–19. This creates narrative consistency and theological clarity: ascent is permitted only after complete withdrawal of divine manifestation. The expansion is exegetical, not lexical.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek adds 'voices, trumpets, and cloud' to clarify that people can approach only after all signs of God's presence have left the mountain.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 308
Rofé, The Belief in Angels in the Bible and in Early Israel, p. 112
revisions.counter_arguments: Could represent a variant Hebrew Vorlage rather than LXX theological expansion.

revisions.mt_reading: The time the Israelites lived in Egypt was 430 years
revisions.revised_reading: The time the Israelites and their fathers lived in Egypt and in the land of Canaan was 430 years
revisions.evidence: The LXX inserts 'and in the land of Canaan' to harmonize the 430-year chronology with Genesis patriarchal narratives. MT's 430 years in Egypt alone conflicts with Gen 15:13 and the four-generation scheme. The LXX solves this by splitting the period between Canaan (patriarchs) and Egypt (bondage), reflecting systematic chronological exegesis.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek adds 'and in the land of Canaan' so the 430 years includes the time of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, solving a math problem between Genesis and Exodus.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 184
Rösel, Übersetzung als Vollendung der Auslegung, p. 78
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX might preserve an early textual tradition also reflected in Samaritan Pentateuch rather than constitute harmonization.

revisions.mt_reading: You shall not revile God (אֱלֹהִים), nor curse a ruler of your people
revisions.revised_reading: You shall not revile the gods (θεούς), nor curse a ruler of your people
revisions.evidence: The LXX translates אֱלֹהִים as plural θεούς, potentially softening the capital offense of blaspheming YHWH to a broader prohibition against reviling any divine beings or judges (cf. Exod 21:6). This interpretive move allows application to pagan contexts and reduces the severity of the law. Philo and Josephus exploit this plural reading for apologetic purposes.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek uses a plural 'gods' instead of singular 'God,' which some interpreters think refers to human judges and makes the law less about blasphemy against God specifically.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, p. 365
revisions.counter_arguments: The plural might be literal rendering of אֱלֹהִים without theological intent, especially if judges are meant.

revisions.mt_reading: YHWH is a man of war (אִישׁ מִלְחָמָה); YHWH is his name
revisions.revised_reading: The Lord is a hero who smashes wars; YHWH is his name
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos avoids אִישׁ ('man') by paraphrasing 'performs mighty deeds,' while Pseudo-Jonathan retains 'hero' (גַבָּר) but adds interpretive gloss 'smashes wars.' Both eliminate the anthropomorphic 'man of war' language. The MT's martial anthropomorphism is systematically revised to emphasize divine acts rather than divine form, consistent with Targumic theology.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic removes the phrase 'man of war' because it makes God sound too human, replacing it with descriptions of God's powerful deeds in battle.
scholarly_citations: Aberbach & Grossfeld, Targum Onkelos to Exodus, p. 91
Shinan & Zakovitch, From Gods to God, p. 156
revisions.counter_arguments: The Targumic rendering might be stylistic elevation rather than strict anthropomorphism avoidance.

dominant_strategy: Anthropomorphism avoidance is the dominant revision strategy in Exodus, particularly in theophanies (Exod 24, 33) and divine-human encounters. Both LXX and Targums systematically buffer direct vision of God, corporeal language, and divine emotions through abstraction, intermediaries (angels, Shekinah), and passive constructions. This reflects Hellenistic-Jewish and proto-rabbinic discomfort with naïve corporeal theism and the scandal of unmediated divine-human contact.
overall_assessment: The pattern of theological revision in Exodus reveals a sustained cross-traditional anxiety about anthropomorphism and divine accessibility, intensifying from Second Temple into early rabbinic periods. LXX translators navigate between literal fidelity and apologetic concerns for Greek-speaking audiences, while Targumic tradents deploy full exegetical arsenal—Shekinah theology, angelology, halakhic precision—to create interpretive distance. Harmonization appears primarily in chronological and legal material (Exod 12:40, 19:13), suggesting tradents were committed to Pentateuchal coherence. The comparative absence of messianic heightening (unlike Psalms or Isaiah) confirms Exodus functioned primarily as constitutional-cultic narrative rather than eschatological resource. The most theologically sensitive material—theophanies and covenant mediation—receives the heaviest revision, indicating that Moses' unparalleled access to God (Num 12:8) remained a hermeneutical problem for subsequent tradents who sought to protect transcendence while preserving narrative intimacy.
overall_plain: Translators of Exodus were most concerned with protecting God's transcendence by softening language that made God seem too physical or too accessible to humans. Greek and Aramaic translators used different techniques—adding intermediaries, changing active to passive verbs, inserting interpretive phrases—but shared the goal of creating reverent distance between God and humanity while keeping Moses' unique relationship with God intact.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Theophanic Mediation: Buffering Divine-Human Contact in Exodus
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The systematic introduction of intermediaries (angels, Shekinah, 'place') and perceptual qualifiers ('glory,' 'back parts') in Exodus theophanies reveals a theological trajectory from narrative immediacy toward metaphysical distance. This is not merely translation caution but a programmatic reinterpretation of Sinai revelation, repositioning Moses not as direct visionary but as uniquely privileged mediator within a graduated ontological hierarchy. The revisions expose deep anxiety about unmediated divine presence, anticipating later rabbinic and Christian mystical traditions that would make theophany the exception requiring elaborate halakhic or sacramental frameworks.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Later translators consistently inserted go-betweens and qualifiers into moments when the text originally described direct encounters between God and humans, showing a major theological shift toward seeing God as more distant and requiring mediators.
